rows.q: In Alphabet's 2023 10-K, roughly what share of total revenues came from online advertising in 2023?

A.answer:
**About 77%**. [goog-20231231.pdf p.36]

- **Google advertising (2023): 237,855** million [goog-20231231.pdf p.36]  
- **Total revenues (2023): 307,394** million [goog-20231231.pdf p.36]  
- **Ratio: 237,855 / 307,394 = 0.7738x** [goog-20231231.pdf p.36]

_Note: I removed one or more statements I could not verify against the source documents. I'm not stating anything I couldn't confirm._

B.answer:
Roughly **77%** of Alphabet’s total revenues came from online advertising in 2023.[goog-20231231.pdf p.36]

Supporting figures:
- **Google advertising:** **237,855** million.[goog-20231231.pdf p.36]
- **Total revenues:** **307,394** million.[goog-20231231.pdf p.36]
- Computed share: **237,855 / 307,394 = 0.7738x**, or about **77%**.[goog-20231231.pdf p.36]

rows.q: In Alphabet's 2023 10-K, how do the rights of the different classes of common/capital stock differ?

A.answer:
The classes differ only in voting rights, plus Class B has a conversion feature. [goog-20231231.pdf p.81]

- “The rights of the holders of each class of our common and capital stock are identical, except with respect to voting.” [goog-20231231.pdf p.81]
- “Each share of Class A common stock is entitled to one vote per share.” [goog-20231231.pdf p.81]
- “Each share of Class B common stock is entitled to 10 votes per share.” [goog-20231231.pdf p.81]
- “Class C capital stock has no voting rights, except as required by applicable law.” [goog-20231231.pdf p.81]
- “Shares of Class B common stock may be converted at any time at the option of the stockholder and automatically convert upon sale or transfer to Class A common stock.” [goog-20231231.pdf p.81]

Also, Alphabet states that the classes have identical dividend and liquidation rights: “The rights, including the liquidation and dividend rights, of the holders of our Class A, Class B, and Class C stock are identical, except with respect to voting.” [goog-20231231.pdf p.81]

B.answer:
Alphabet’s 2023 10-K says the stock classes differ primarily in **voting rights**: “Our Class B stock has 10 votes per share, our Class A stock has one vote per share, and our Class C stock has no voting rights.” [goog-20231231.pdf p.23]

The filing also states that **Class C is generally non-voting except as required by law**: “because our Class C stock carries no voting rights (except as required by applicable law)” [goog-20231231.pdf p.23]

So, in short:
- **Class B common stock:** 10 votes per share [goog-20231231.pdf p.23]
- **Class A common stock:** 1 vote per share [goog-20231231.pdf p.23]
- **Class C capital stock:** no voting rights, except as required by applicable law [goog-20231231.pdf p.23]

I could verify the voting-rights distinction from the 10-K, but I could not verify any further class-specific differences from the materials I was able to access in this vault.
